text: What is the main role of the R group in an amino acid?
choices: It forms the sugar-phosphate backbone of DNA
It determines the unique chemical properties of the amino acid
It links amino acids together with hydrogen bonds
It stores hereditary information
explain: The R group (side chain) gives each amino acid its characteristic chemical behavior.

text: What is the main role of phosphodiester bonds in nucleic acids?
choices: They join complementary bases across two DNA strands
They link nucleotides together within a nucleic acid strand
They connect amino acids in proteins
They attach fatty acids to glycerol
explain: Phosphodiester bonds connect the sugar and phosphate groups of adjacent nucleotides in a strand.

text: What is the main reason unsaturated fats are often liquid at room temperature?
choices: Their double bonds create bends that prevent tight packing
They are made of amino acids instead of fatty acids
They contain no carbon-hydrogen bonds
They are strongly ionic
explain: Double bonds introduce kinks that reduce packing and lower melting point.

text: A cell contains an enzyme that functions in the aqueous cytoplasm. Which amino acid property would be most favorable for many residues exposed on the enzyme’s surface?
choices: Nonpolar side chains that avoid water
Polar or charged side chains that interact favorably with water
Side chains that can only form covalent bonds
Side chains containing no atoms other than carbon
explain: Surface-exposed residues that are polar or charged interact well with water and stabilize soluble proteins.

text: A scientist constructs two carbohydrate polymers from glucose. One polymer is highly branched and rapidly broken down for energy release. The other is unbranched and used for structure. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The arrangement of glucose monomers influences the molecule’s function
Glucose can form only one biologically useful polymer
Structural carbohydrates must contain amino acids
Highly branched carbohydrates cannot be used for energy storage
explain: Linkage patterns and branching determine polymer properties like accessibility to enzymes and structural rigidity.

text: A mutation changes one amino acid from hydrophobic to hydrophilic in the core of a protein. Which outcome is most likely?
choices: The protein may fold less stably because the hydrophobic interior is disrupted
The protein will automatically become a carbohydrate
The protein must gain additional amino acids
The protein will definitely function better
explain: Introducing a polar residue into a hydrophobic core disrupts packing and can destabilize folding.

text: A researcher breaks a nucleic acid polymer into its monomers and finds that each monomer contains a phosphate group, a five-carbon sugar, and a nitrogenous base. Which conclusion is best?
choices: The polymer was a protein
The polymer was a nucleic acid made of nucleotides
The polymer was a lipid bilayer
The polymer was a carbohydrate made of glucose
explain: Those three components define a nucleotide, the monomer of nucleic acids.

text: A molecule with many hydroxyl groups dissolves well in water. What is the best explanation?
choices: Hydroxyl groups make the molecule more able to form hydrogen bonds with water
Hydroxyl groups remove all partial charges from the molecule
Water dissolves only molecules that contain peptide bonds
Molecules with hydroxyl groups are always lipids
explain: Hydroxyls increase polarity and hydrogen-bonding capacity, improving water solubility.

text: A phospholipid differs from a triglyceride primarily because the phospholipid has which feature?
choices: A large carbohydrate chain
A hydrophilic region that allows interaction with water
Amino acid monomers joined by peptide bonds
A sequence of nitrogenous bases
explain: Phospholipids have a polar (phosphate-containing) head that interacts with water, enabling membrane formation.

text: A scientist compares DNA sample X and DNA sample Y. Sample X has more G-C pairs and resists strand separation more strongly. Which statement best explains this?
choices: G-C pairs contribute more hydrogen bonds than A-T pairs
G-C pairs are covalently bonded across the strands
A-T pairs contain amino acids instead of bases
G-C rich DNA must be single-stranded
explain: G-C base pairs form three hydrogen bonds (vs two for A-T), increasing duplex stability.

text: A student says, “Buffers make pH changes impossible.” Which response is best?
choices: Correct, because buffers keep pH permanently fixed at one value
Incorrect, because buffers only resist sudden or large pH changes
Correct, because buffers remove all hydrogen ions from solution
Incorrect, because buffers affect only temperature and not pH
explain: Buffers resist pH change within a range but can be overwhelmed; they don't make pH immutable.

text: A protein is moved from an aqueous environment into the hydrophobic interior of a membrane. Which surface change would likely make the protein more stable in the membrane?
choices: More charged amino acids exposed on the surface
More nonpolar amino acids exposed on the surface
More phosphate groups added to the outside
Replacement of peptide bonds with hydrogen bonds
explain: Exposing nonpolar residues increases compatibility with the hydrophobic membrane interior.

text: A lipid sample with many double bonds in its fatty acid tails melts at a lower temperature than a sample with mostly single bonds. Why?
choices: Double bonds increase branching of peptide chains
Double bonds create kinks that reduce tight packing of the tails
Double bonds make the molecules strongly hydrophilic
Double bonds remove all carbon from the molecules
explain: Kinks from double bonds reduce van der Waals packing, lowering melting temperature.

text: A mutation changes a codon, but the resulting protein keeps the same amino acid at that position. Which explanation is most likely?
choices: The mutation was silent because multiple codons can code for the same amino acid
The mutation must have occurred in a lipid
The mutation changed the protein but not the DNA
The mutation removed a peptide bond from the protein
explain: Redundancy in the genetic code allows synonymous codons that do not alter the amino acid sequence.

text: A researcher compares two enzymes with identical amino acid length. One works at neutral pH, while the other loses function in acidic conditions. Which explanation is most reasonable?
choices: Differences in amino acid composition likely cause different responses to proton concentration
Enzymes are unaffected by pH
Acidic conditions convert proteins into carbohydrates
Neutral pH destroys all enzyme structure
explain: pH affects ionization of side chains and can alter structure and activity depending on composition.

text: A carbohydrate polymer and a protein are both broken into monomers. Why are the resulting monomers different?
choices: Different biological polymers are made from different types of monomers
All polymers are built from the same monomer type
Proteins are not actually polymers
Carbohydrates are made of nucleotides
explain: Carbohydrates are made of sugars; proteins are made of amino acids — different monomer classes.

text: A biologist finds an unknown molecule that is rich in carbon-hydrogen bonds and mostly nonpolar. Which biological role is most likely?
choices: Long-term energy storage
Immediate storage of genetic information as a nucleotide sequence
Formation of the sugar-phosphate backbone of DNA
Maintaining high solubility in water
explain: Nonpolar C-H rich molecules (like many lipids) are well-suited for energy storage due to high chemical energy content.

text: A researcher builds two proteins using the same amino acids but in different sequences. Why can the proteins behave differently?
choices: Amino acid order influences folding and therefore shape and function
Sequence order matters only in carbohydrates, not in proteins
Proteins with the same amino acids always have identical function
The different sequences automatically create nucleic acids
explain: Primary sequence directs folding pathways and ultimately determines structure and function.

text:
Students compare two samples after identical heating.

Sample A temperature change: +5°C
Sample B temperature change: +16°C

Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Sample A likely has a higher specific heat than Sample B
Sample B absorbed no energy
Sample A must contain DNA
Sample B must be basic
explain: Under equal energy input, a smaller temperature change indicates a higher specific heat capacity.

text:
Researchers test an enzyme at different pH values and observe the following:

pH 3: very low activity
pH 5: moderate activity
pH 7: highest activity
pH 10: very low activity

Which interpretation is best?
choices: The enzyme functions best near neutral pH, and extreme pH likely disrupts interactions important for its structure or active site
The enzyme becomes more active every time pH rises
The enzyme is a carbohydrate because pH affects it
pH affects only the substrate and not the enzyme itself
explain: Enzymes have pH optima where side-chain ionization states favor correct folding and catalysis.

text: A mutation changes one amino acid in a protein from cysteine to serine. The protein becomes less stable, but its length is unchanged. Which explanation is most likely?
choices: The change may have removed a possible disulfide bond that helped stabilize the protein
The mutation must have removed the entire primary structure
The protein became a nucleic acid
Serine breaks peptide bonds automatically
explain: Cysteine can form disulfide bonds that stabilize structure; replacing it with serine removes that covalent stabilizer.

text: Students place phospholipids into water and observe vesicle formation. They place triglycerides into water and observe separate droplets. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Amphipathic molecules can self-organize into membrane-like structures, while mostly nonpolar lipids tend to separate
Triglycerides are made of amino acids, so they cannot form droplets
Phospholipids dissolve into individual nucleotides in water
Water becomes nonpolar in the presence of phospholipids
explain: Phospholipids have hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails, promoting bilayer or vesicle assembly; triglycerides form droplets.

text: A scientist studies two proteins with the same length. Protein A has many hydrophilic residues on its surface and is found in cytoplasm. Protein B has many hydrophobic residues on its surface and is embedded in a membrane. What principle is best illustrated?
choices: Surface amino acid properties that match the environment help determine protein stability and function
All proteins with the same length must have the same function
Membrane proteins are made from lipids rather than amino acids
Cytoplasmic proteins cannot contain any nonpolar amino acids
explain: Proteins evolve surface chemistries suited to their environments; hydrophobic surfaces favor membrane embedding.
